question: A 66-year-old diabetic woman presents with abdominal pain. CT imaging is obtained (image shown). Which initial antimicrobial regimen is most appropriate?

options.D: No antibiotics; proceed to drainage only

ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the CT images closely. All three panels show focal collections of gas within the affected right kidney: small intrapelvic/collecting-system gas bubbles on the axial images and additional gas tracking into the renal parenchyma and perinephric region on the coronal view (arrows). These imaging features indicate a gas‑forming, destructive renal infection with extension beyond the collecting system.

Next, I relate the imaging to the management choices in the question. The patient is an older diabetic with radiologic evidence of an aggressive renal infection that has produced intrarenal and perinephric gas — a situation that calls for prompt systemic therapy in addition to any procedural drainage. Therefore an initial oral regimen is unlikely to be adequate, and withholding antibiotics while proceeding to drainage alone would leave a systemic/organ‑threatening infection untreated.

Then I compare the specific options against those observations. Oral ciprofloxacin (B) provides only oral therapy and is therefore not appropriate as the initial single measure for this severe, gas‑forming renal infection. IV vancomycin alone (C) would not address a broad spectrum of likely pathogens responsible for a destructive renal/paranephric infection. IV piperacillin–tazobactam (A) is an intravenous, broad‑spectrum antimicrobial choice that is appropriate as initial empiric therapy for a severe renal infection with gas formation while drainage/definitive source control is arranged.

In summary, given the CT evidence of an aggressive gas‑forming renal and perinephric infection in a diabetic patient, the most appropriate initial antimicrobial regimen among the options is IV piperacillin–tazobactam.
caption: CT staging of emphysematous pyelonephritis. a Axial oblique MPR reconstruction of the parenchymal phase of a 53-year-old diabetic female patient presenting with mild fever shows a class I EPN, characterized by gas only inside collecting system, in association with the presence of a calculus in the renal pelvis (arrow). b Axial scan of the parenchymal phase of a 73-year-old diabetic female patient presenting with right flank discomfort shows two calyceal calculi with initial parenchyma spread of gas (arrow) in a class II EPN. c Coronal oblique MPR reconstruction of a 66-year-old diabetic female patient presenting with abdominal pain shows a class III EPN with a voluminous hypodense fluid-gaseous abscess filled with gas bubbles, dissecting the interstitial space and invading the perinephric space (arrow)
sub_caption: c Coronal oblique MPR reconstruction of a 66-year-old diabetic female patient presenting with abdominal pain shows a class III EPN with a voluminous hypodense fluid-gaseous abscess filled with gas bubbles, dissecting the interstitial space and invading the perinephric space (arrow)
image_context: Subsequently, Huang et al. [49] proposed four classes of EPN based on CT scan, also providing a prognostic stratification, essential for patient’s management (Table 3). In particular, in class 1 gas is present only in the collecting system (Fig. <xref rid="11547_2020_1315_Fig5_HTML" ref-type="fig">5</xref>a), in class 2 gas is present in the renal parenchyma without extension to the extrarenal area (Fig. a), in class 2 gas is present in the renal parenchyma without extension to the extrarenal area (Fig. <xref rid="11547_2020_1315_Fig5_HTML" ref-type="fig">5</xref>b), in class 3A gas and or abscess are present in the perinephric area (Fig. b), in class 3A gas and or abscess are present in the perinephric area (Fig. <xref rid="11547_2020_1315_Fig5_HTML" ref-type="fig">5</xref>c), in class 3B gas or abscess are present in the pararenal space, while in class 4 EPN is bilateral. Although no significant differences were noted in clinical features among the 4 classes, mortality progressively increases, with class 4 being the worst. In particular, class 1 and 2 patients can be treated with percutaneous drainage combined with antibiotics, as well as patients with more extensive EPN, such as class 3A and 3B, on condition there are < 2 risk factors (i.e., thrombocytopenia, acute renal function impairment, disturbance of consciousness, and shock). Nephrectomy should be promptly performed in class 3A and 3B with > 2 risk factors and in class 4 cases; however, given the instability of class 4 patients, bilateral percutaneous drainage should be tried first, in order to avoid an emergency nephrectomy [c), in class 3B gas or abscess are present in the pararenal space, while in class 4 EPN is bilateral. Although no significant differences were noted in clinical features among the 4 classes, mortality progressively increases, with class 4 being the worst. In particular, class 1 and 2 patients can be treated with percutaneous drainage combined with antibiotics, as well as patients with more extensive EPN, such as class 3A and 3B, on condition there are < 2 risk factors (i.e., thrombocytopenia, acute renal function impairment, disturbance of consciousness, and shock). Nephrectomy should be promptly performed in class 3A and 3B with > 2 risk factors and in class 4 cases; however, given the instability of class 4 patients, bilateral percutaneous drainage should be tried first, in order to avoid an emergency nephrectomy [53].Table 3CT staging of emphysematous pyelonephritisClassificationClassWan et al.
